event: Pagan Ritual of the Daughters of the Philistines
symbols: golden idol on pedestal — the false gods of the nations, the abomination set up in opposition to the true God of Israel
dancing women — the seductive power of pagan worship that lured Israel into apostasy throughout the period of the Judges
night setting — spiritual darkness and the blindness of idolatry
raised arms — ecstatic pagan devotion contrasted with the ordered worship commanded by God
solitary robed figure — possibly a priestess or observer, the institutional structure of false religion
description: A group of women in vibrant, loosely draped garments dance and prostrate themselves before a golden idol set on a raised pedestal. The scene is bathed in warm amber and ochre tones, with a deep blue-violet night sky behind a fence or palisade. Several women raise their arms in ecstasy, others kneel or bow low. A solitary robed figure stands apart at the right edge. The style is boldly colorful and flat, with strong outlines in a Post-Impressionist manner.
theological_description: This image depicts the cultic worship of the Philistine women, evoking the pervasive idolatry that Israel was commanded to reject. The Philistines worshipped Dagon (Judges 16:23; 1 Samuel 5:1-7) and Baal-Zebub (2 Kings 1:2), and their women were instruments of temptation — Samson's entanglement with Philistine women led to his downfall (Judges 14-16). The scene echoes the golden calf apostasy (Exodus 32:6, 19) where Israel danced before an idol, and the Moabite women at Baal-Peor who seduced Israel into pagan worship (Numbers 25:1-3). Throughout the Deuteronomistic history, the 'daughters of foreign nations' symbolize the danger of religious syncretism that provokes divine jealousy (Deuteronomy 7:3-4). The prophets consistently use the imagery of idolatrous worship as spiritual adultery (Hosea 2; Ezekiel 16), a betrayal of the covenant marriage between God and His people.

scripture_refs.reason: The Philistines gathered to offer sacrifice to Dagon their god — the cultic context of Philistine worship.
The idol Dagon falling before the Ark of the Covenant, demonstrating the impotence of Philistine gods.
The people sat down to eat and drink and rose up to play — idolatrous revelry echoed in this scene.
Israel joined in worship of Baal-Peor through the daughters of Moab — foreign women and idolatry intertwined.
The prohibition against intermarriage with pagan nations lest they turn Israel's heart to other gods.
Samson's desire for a Philistine woman, beginning the pattern of entanglement with Philistine culture.
Israel's harlotry with idols, the prophetic metaphor for exactly this kind of pagan worship.
